unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you will explore the many different types of stories that writers create. Every story belongs to a particular genre, which is the French word for type or kind. By the end of this unit you will understand several genres, recognise their special features, and be able to choose the right genre for your own writing. You will also strengthen your grammar by learning how to connect ideas smoothly using connectives and conjunctions.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Discuss evidence and language choices. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise two to three paragraphs. Present, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners exploring story genres and planning original writing

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Summarise the central ideas and important details in A Different Type of Story texts.
Support inferences and comparisons with relevant text evidence.
Use Grade 5 vocabulary accurately in discussion and writing.
Apply the unit language focus in connected sentences and paragraphs.
Create two to three organised paragraphs for a defined purpose and audience.
Present a clear three-minute response and answer follow-up questions.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: A Different Type of Story: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In this unit you will explore the many different types of stories that writers create. Every story belongs to a particular genre, which is the French word for type or kind. By the end of this unit you will understand several genres, recognise their special features, and be able to choose the right genre for your own writing. You will also strengthen your grammar by learning how to connect ideas smoothly using connectives and conjunctions.
Identify and describe at least five different story genres: adventure, mystery, fable, legend, and realistic fiction
Explain the key features that make each genre different from the others
Read a passage that introduces multiple genres and answer questions about it at different thinking levels
Use coordinating conjunctions (the FANBOYS words) to join ideas of equal importance
Use subordinating conjunctions such as because, although, when, if, and while to show how ideas relate
Use connective adverbs such as however, therefore, and meanwhile to link sentences smoothly
Plan and write the opening of a story in a genre of your choice, using a planning frame
Take part in speaking and listening activities including a genre detective game and a book recommendation talk
Evaluate your own writing and a partner's writing using a peer-feedback checklist
Reflect on your learning by completing a set of self-assessment statements
Before you begin reading, study the words below. These words will appear throughout the unit. Make sure you understand each one so that you can follow the texts and use the words in your own writing.
Try to use at least five of these words in your writing tasks later in the unit. The more you practise, the more naturally these words will come to you.
Read the following passage carefully. It is set in a school library where a librarian is speaking to a group of Year Five learners about different types of stories. As you read, think about the features of each genre that the librarian describes.

readings.title: A Different Type of Story: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"Welcome, everyone, to our school library," said Mrs Ishmael, spreading her arms wide. She stood in front of five colourful bookshelves, each one decorated with a different banner. "Today I am going to take you on a special tour. We are not going to walk through the library. Instead, we are going to travel through the world of story genres. Each of these five shelves holds a different type of story, and by the end of our tour you will know exactly which shelf to visit when you want a particular kind of reading experience."
She walked to the first shelf, which was decorated with a bright orange banner showing a compass and a map. "This is our adventure shelf," she said. "Adventure stories take characters on exciting journeys. The characters often face dangerous situations, solve problems, and discover new places. Think of a story where a young sailor must cross the Red Sea during a terrible storm, or where a group of friends travel through the Somali highlands searching for a hidden waterfall. In adventure stories, the setting changes often, and the plot is full of action. The main character is usually brave, although they might feel afraid at first. What makes adventure stories special is that they keep you turning the pages because you always want to know what happens next."
Mrs Ishmael moved to the second shelf. Its banner was dark blue, covered with magnifying glasses and question marks. "This is the mystery shelf," she explained. "In a mystery, something puzzling happens at the beginning of the story, and the characters spend the rest of the story trying to work it out. Perhaps a valuable book has gone missing from the school library, or a strange light appears on the beach every Thursday night. Mystery stories are built on suspense. The writer gives you clues along the way, but also includes false clues to keep you guessing. A good mystery makes you feel like a detective yourself, and when the answer is finally revealed, everything suddenly makes sense."

readings.title: The Case of the Incomplete Rule, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The morning air in Bukoba town was heavy. Clouds hovered like thoughts waiting to fall. Inside the small red-brick courthouse, children from Liberty Primary School took their seats. They were here for a role-play activity — part of their civic education week.
Ishmael adjusted her papers. “I’m the lawyer,” she whispered to Musa, who played the judge. “Let’s make this trial meaningful.”
“It already is,” Musa said. “We’re discussing a real law that was recently dismantled — one that caused a lot of discrimination.”
As the fake trial began, the classroom grew quiet. A teacher read from an old dusty passage:
“This rule denied students with a disability from attending top schools unless they passed a different exam.”
Ishmael stood up. “This is not just unfair. It’s injustice,” she said firmly. “It shows clear disregard for fairness.”
“Objection!” shouted Sophia, the pretend prosecutor. “This law was not created to harm anyone!”
“But it did,” Ibrahim replied. “Ask any student who felt unwelcome, uneasy, or ignored.”
After the trial, the students gathered in the library. “We need to understand how these rules were made,” said Musa.
They opened old files from the education office. Some were illegible, written in faded ink. Others were full of stamps and long paragraphs.
“Look at this one,” said Sophia. “It’s full of crossed-out sections. It’s clearly incomplete.”
Musa pointed to a word. “And this part says the rule was reviewed during a time of civil unrest.”
They flipped pages, curious and focused. But Ibrahim stopped at a folder marked ‘Unheard Voices’.
Inside were letters from students across the country. One line read:
“I feel like I’m being punished for a condition I can’t change.”
Ishmael sat down slowly. “These students never had a chance to speak in court, did they?”

readings.title: The Case of the Incomplete Rule, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Musa shook his head. “They wrote letters instead of giving speeches.”
Sophia added, “Maybe because they thought no one would listen.”
“We’re listening now,” said Ibrahim, her voice firm. “And that matters.”
The group grew quiet again, not from sadness, but from understanding.
They weren’t just reading — they were witnessing something that should have never been forgotten.
“That rule didn’t just block schools,” Musa said finally. “It blocked hope.”
Later that day, the teacher led a group discussion.
“Why do you think this law lasted so long?” she asked.
“Because people were unaware of its effects,” said Musa.
“Because some were too uncertain to speak,” added Sophia.
Ishmael raised her hand. “And some felt incapable of changing the system.”
“That’s a powerful reflection,” the teacher said. “And what helped change it?”
The next day, it rained hard. Power flickered. But in the back of the school, the students worked by window light. They were making a timeline of events.
“This part shows when the law was passed,” said Musa.
“And this is when protests began,” added Sophia.
Ishmael drew a scale of justice. “This symbolises what we must protect — even when things feel monotonous or slow.”
Sophia grinned. “Your drawing is as clear as a gavel’s knock.”
“And your timeline is as navigable as a city map,” said Ibrahim.
At the end of the week, they held a school-wide debate.
Ishmael opened her notes. “This law caused disadvantage,” she said. “It may have been legal, but it wasn’t just.”
Sophia stood. “Unfortunately,” she began, “we didn’t fix it sooner.”
“Fortunately,” she added, “we’re speaking now.”
The headteacher, who watched silently, stood up. “You children have reminded us of something important,” she said. “Old laws are not always right. Some must be questioned. Some must be changed.”

grammar.title: What Are Connectives and Conjunctions?
grammar.explanation:
A connective is any word or phrase that links ideas in your writing. A conjunction is a specific type of connective that joins words, phrases, or clauses within a sentence. People often use the two words to mean the same thing.
Why do they matter? Without connectives, your writing would be a list of short, choppy sentences. Connectives make your ideas flow and help the reader follow your thinking.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
A connective is any word or phrase that links ideas in your writing. A conjunction is a specific type of connective that joins words, phrases, or clauses within a sentence. People often use the two words to mean the same thing.
Why do they matter? Without connectives, your writing would be a list of short, choppy sentences. Connectives make your ideas flow and help the reader follow your thinking.
Without connectives:
"The rule was unfair. The students protested. The rule was changed."
With connectives:
"The rule was unfair, so the students protested, and eventually it was changed."
See how the second version reads more smoothly? That is the power of connectives.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are connectives and conjunctions? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Coordinating Conjunctions (FANBOYS)
grammar.explanation:
Coordinating conjunctions join two ideas of equal importance. The easiest way to remember them is the acronym FANBOYS:
Punctuation Rule
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Coordinating conjunctions join two ideas of equal importance. The easiest way to remember them is the acronym FANBOYS:
Punctuation Rule
When a FANBOYS conjunction joins two complete sentences, put a comma before it:
"The trial began , and the room grew quiet." (two complete ideas — comma needed)
"Ibrahim read and shared the letters." (one subject, two actions — no comma needed)
Practice 1 — FANBOYS
Choose the best FANBOYS conjunction to fill each gap.
I wanted to help, ______ I did not know what to do.
You can come with us, ______ you can stay at home.
The students were tired, ______ they kept working.
She studied every day, ______ she wanted to pass the exam.
He did not read the notice, ______ did he ask about it.
They found old files, ______ many of them were illegible.
The rule was changed, ______ the students had spoken up.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply coordinating conjunctions (fanboys) in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Subordinating Conjunctions
grammar.explanation:
Subordinating conjunctions join a main clause (the main idea) with a subordinate clause (extra information that depends on the main idea). The subordinate clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence.
Common Subordinating Conjunctions
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Subordinating conjunctions join a main clause (the main idea) with a subordinate clause (extra information that depends on the main idea). The subordinate clause cannot stand alone as a complete sentence.
Common Subordinating Conjunctions
Punctuation Rule
When the subordinate clause comes first, put a comma after it:
"Because the rule was unfair , they protested." (subordinate clause first — comma needed)
"They protested because the rule was unfair." (main clause first — no comma needed)
Practice 2 — Subordinating Conjunctions
Fill in each blank with the best subordinating conjunction.
______ the students found the letters, they felt heartbroken.
Ishmael spoke firmly ______ she believed in justice.
______ it rained heavily, the students kept working by window light.
They could not read the documents ______ the ink had faded.
______ you notice unfairness, you should speak up.
Musa searched the files ______ he found the folder marked 'Unheard Voices'.
______ the debate began, the headteacher sat in silence.
The law remained ______ brave students challenged it.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply subordinating conjunctions in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Connective Adverbs (Linking Words Between Sentences)
grammar.explanation:
Connective adverbs are words that link separate sentences rather than clauses within one sentence. They are usually followed by a comma.
Practice 3 — Connective Adverbs
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Connective adverbs are words that link separate sentences rather than clauses within one sentence. They are usually followed by a comma.
Practice 3 — Connective Adverbs
Choose the best connective adverb to link these sentence pairs.
The students worked hard all week. ______, their debate was a great success.
The old files were full of stamps and long paragraphs. ______, some sections were illegible.
Sophia believed the law was not meant to harm anyone. ______, Ibrahim showed that it did.
______, the power went out during the rainstorm.
The headteacher was impressed by the students. ______, she asked them to present at assembly.
Musa organised the timeline. ______, Ibrahim prepared her closing speech.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply connective adverbs (linking words between sentences) in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Time Connectives for Storytelling
grammar.explanation:
When you retell a story or write one of your own, time connectives help the reader follow the order of events:
First / Firstly — the start of the sequence
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
When you retell a story or write one of your own, time connectives help the reader follow the order of events:
First / Firstly — the start of the sequence
Then / Next / After that — what comes in the middle
Meanwhile / At the same time — things happening together
Later / Eventually — after some time has passed
Finally / In the end — the last event
Practice 4 — Retell in Order
Put these events from the story in order. Then rewrite them as a paragraph using time connectives (first, then, next, after that, finally).
The students held a school-wide debate.
The children did a role-play trial in the courthouse.
Ishmael wrote in her journal about what she had learned.
The students found letters in a folder marked 'Unheard Voices'.
The headteacher praised the students and read their slogan.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply time connectives for storytelling in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Mistake 1: Starting a sentence with 'And' or 'But'
In formal writing, avoid starting sentences with coordinating conjunctions. Use a connective adverb instead:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Mistake 1: Starting a sentence with 'And' or 'But'
In formal writing, avoid starting sentences with coordinating conjunctions. Use a connective adverb instead:
Informal: "And they found more letters." → Better: "Furthermore, they found more letters."
Informal: "But it was still unfair." → Better: "However, it was still unfair."
(Note: In creative writing and stories, starting with 'And' or 'But' is acceptable for effect.)
Mistake 2: Using 'because' at the start without finishing the thought
Wrong: "Because it rained." (This is not a complete sentence.)
Right: "Because it rained, they worked inside." or "They worked inside because it rained."
Mistake 3: Mixing up 'but' and 'so'
but = contrast (two different ideas): "The law helped, but it was incomplete."
so = result (one idea causes the other): "The law was incomplete, so they challenged it."
Mistake 4: Missing commas
Remember: use a comma before FANBOYS when joining two complete sentences, and use a comma after a subordinate clause that comes first.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Genre Detective Game
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: In this game, one person describes a story without saying which genre it belongs to. The listener must guess the genre by paying close attention to the clues. Here is how to play. First, the speaker thinks of a story they know, whether it is a book they have read, a film they have watched, or a story someone told them. Second, the speaker describes the story in three or four sentences, mentioning details about the characters, the setting, and the plot, but never naming the genre. Third, the listener guesses the genre and explains which clues helped them decide. Fourth, swap roles and play again. Try to play at least four rounds so that you cover different genres. After each round, discuss whether the clues were clear enough or whether the speaker needed to include more genre-specific features.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise genre detective game and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Book Recommendation Talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Prepare a short talk of about one to two minutes in which you recommend a story to a friend. Your talk should include the following points. Name the story and its author if you know it. Tell your listener which genre the story belongs to and why. Describe the main character and the setting in a few sentences without giving away the ending. Explain why you enjoyed the story and why you think your listener would enjoy it too. Use at least two connectives to link your ideas, for example, 'Although the beginning is slow, the story becomes very exciting in the middle.' Practise your talk before you deliver it. Speak clearly, make eye contact, and try to show enthusiasm for the story you are recommending.
Activity 3 — Group Story Starter
This is a collaborative storytelling activity. You need at least two people, but three or four is even better. First, the group agrees on a genre. Second, the first person says one or two opening sentences that fit the genre. For example, if the group chooses adventure, the first person might say: 'The boat rocked dangerously as the storm rolled in from the east.' Third, the next person continues the story by adding one or two more sentences, using at least one connective. Fourth, keep going around the group, each person adding to the story. Try to keep the story in the same genre throughout. After five or six turns, end the story together by agreeing on a final sentence. Afterwards, discuss how well the group kept to the genre. Did anyone accidentally shift into a different genre? What connectives did the group use? This activity helps you think on your feet and practise using connectives in spoken language.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise book recommendation talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Dramatic Reading
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a short passage from a story, either the passage about Mrs Ishmael's library tour from this unit or a passage from another story you enjoy. Practise reading it aloud with expression. Think about the following points as you prepare. First, vary your pace. Read exciting parts a little faster and thoughtful or suspenseful parts more slowly. Second, change your volume. If a character whispers, lower your voice. If a character shouts, raise it. Third, use pauses. A short pause before an important word or after a dramatic event creates suspense and lets the listener absorb what has happened. Fourth, if the passage includes direct speech, try to give each character a slightly different voice or tone. Perform your dramatic reading for a family member or partner. After your performance, ask your listener which part they found most interesting or dramatic and why.
Check Your Learning
Now that you have completed this unit, read each statement below and decide honestly whether you can do it. If you can, put a tick. If you are not sure, go back and review the relevant section before moving on. Being honest with yourself is the best way to make progress.
I can name and describe at least five different story genres: adventure, mystery, fable, legend, and realistic fiction.
I can identify which genre a story belongs to by looking at its features, such as the type of characters, the setting, and the plot.
I can explain what a moral is and give an example from a fable.
I can explain the difference between a legend and a fable.
I can use coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS) correctly to join two ideas of equal importance.
I can use subordinating conjunctions such as because, although, when, if, unless, while, until, after, before, and since to link a main clause with a subordinate clause.
I can use connective adverbs such as however, therefore, meanwhile, and furthermore to link sentences and show the relationship between ideas.
I can punctuate sentences correctly when using conjunctions and connective adverbs, including placing commas in the right positions.
I can plan a story opening using a planning frame that includes genre, character, setting, and opening hook.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise dramatic reading and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Write a Story Opening
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Now it is time to put everything you have learned into practice. In this workshop you will plan and write the opening of a story in a genre of your choice. A good story opening does three important things: it introduces the main character, it establishes the setting, and it hooks the reader so that they want to keep reading. You will write between one and two paragraphs, which should be roughly 100 to 150 words.
Step 1 — Choose Your Genre
Decide which genre you want to write in. Think about the features of each one that you learned from Mrs Ishmael's library tour. Use the descriptions below to help you decide.
Adventure: Your character goes on an exciting journey. The setting changes. There is danger or a challenge to overcome.
Mystery: Something puzzling happens. Your character must find clues. You build suspense by keeping the answer hidden.
Fable: Your main characters are animals who talk and behave like humans. The story is short and teaches a moral lesson.
Legend: Your story is set in the distant past. The main character performs a brave or extraordinary deed. The story feels traditional.
Realistic fiction: Your character is an ordinary person in an everyday situation. The events could really happen. The emotions feel true to life.
Step 2 — Plan Your Opening
Use the planning frame below to organise your ideas before you start writing. You do not need to write in full sentences at this stage. Short notes are fine.
Genre: Write the genre you have chosen.
Main character: Write the character's name, age, and one or two details about their personality or appearance.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about write a story opening.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'unrest' mean?
quizzes.options: A situation where people are unhappy and may protest or cause trouble. | Not achieving the desired result or goal. | Not known or recognised; something you have not seen before. | Something that happens suddenly and is not planned.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A situation where people are unhappy and may protest or cause trouble.
quizzes.explanation: There was unrest in the city because people wanted to change the rules.

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'uneasy' mean?
quizzes.options: Feeling worried or uncomfortable about something. | Not achieving the desired result or goal. | Not known or recognised; something you have not seen before. | Not sure about something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Feeling worried or uncomfortable about something.
quizzes.explanation: I felt uneasy when I heard a strange noise in the dark.

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'unexpected' mean?
quizzes.options: Not knowing or realising something. | Not known or recognised; something you have not seen before. | Not sure about something. | Something that happens suddenly and is not planned.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something that happens suddenly and is not planned.
quizzes.explanation: The rain was unexpected, so we got wet without our umbrellas.

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'unsuccessful' mean?
quizzes.options: Not achieving the desired result or goal. | Not awake and not aware of what is happening. | Not knowing or realising something. | Not sure about something.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not achieving the desired result or goal.
quizzes.explanation: He tried to climb the tree, but he was unsuccessful and fell down.

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: What does 'unfamiliar' mean?
quizzes.options: Not awake and not aware of what is happening. | Not knowing or realising something. | Not known or recognised; something you have not seen before. | One of a kind; very special.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Not known or recognised; something you have not seen before.
quizzes.explanation: The new school was unfamiliar to me, so I felt a little nervous.

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Case of the Incomplete Rule, part 1': “And this part says the rule was reviewed during a time of civil _____.” They flipped pages, curious and focused.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “And this part says the rule was reviewed during a time of civil unrest.” They flipped pages, curious and focused.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Case of the Incomplete Rule, part 1': “Ask any student who felt unwelcome, _____, or ignored.” After the trial, the students gathered in the library.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Ask any student who felt unwelcome, uneasy, or ignored.” After the trial, the students gathered in the library.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Case of the Incomplete Rule, part 2': “Because some were too _____ to speak,” added Sophia.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Because some were too uncertain to speak,” added Sophia.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Case of the Incomplete Rule, part 2': “Because people were _____ of its effects,” said Musa.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Because people were unaware of its effects,” said Musa.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.quizTitle: A Different Type of Story checkpoint
quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Case of the Incomplete Rule, part 2': “_____,” she began, “we didn’t fix it sooner.” “Fortunately,” she added, “we’re speaking now.” The headteacher, who watched silently, stood up.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Unfortunately,” she began, “we didn’t fix it sooner.” “Fortunately,” she added, “we’re speaking now.” The headteacher, who watched silently, stood up.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

liveSessions.title: A Different Type of Story: Launch, vocabulary and learning goals

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between a legend and a myth?
Give me an example of a fable from East African tradition and tell me its moral.
What makes realistic fiction different from a true story or autobiography?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my five sentences from Exercise 5 and tell me if I used the connectives correctly?
Give me three more subordinating conjunctions that I have not learned yet and show me how to use them.
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
I am not sure about some of my Check Your Learning answers. Can you quiz me on the five genres to help me check my understanding?
Can you give me a short passage and ask me to identify which connectives are used and what type each one is?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you test me on the un- words?
Can you give me more words that begin with un-?
Can you help me use 'unfortunately' in a sentence?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain the difference between dis- and un-?
Can you help me spell 'discrimination' correctly?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you give me examples of words that use im-, il-, or ir- instead of in-?
Can you test me on the in- words?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you help me understand the difference between -ible and -able?
Can you give me a spelling test on these long words?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers and explain any I got wrong?
Can you give me more sentences to practise with?
What is the difference between 'although' and 'however'?
Can you help me use connectives in my own writing?

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A situation where people are unhappy and may protest or cause trouble. There was unrest in the city because people wanted to change the rules.
Feeling worried or uncomfortable about something. I felt uneasy when I heard a strange noise in the dark.
Something that happens suddenly and is not planned. The rain was unexpected, so we got wet without our umbrellas.
Not achieving the desired result or goal. He tried to climb the tree, but he was unsuccessful and fell down.
Not known or recognised; something you have not seen before. The new school was unfamiliar to me, so I felt a little nervous.
Not sure about something. I felt uncertain about which game to play.
Not knowing or realising something. She was unaware that her friend needed help.
Not awake and not aware of what is happening. After falling off his bike, he was unconscious and needed help.
One of a kind; very special. My grandma has a unique necklace that no one else has.
Used to say that something is sad or disappointing. Unfortunately, it started to rain during our picnic.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name and describe at least five different story genres: adventure, mystery, fable, legend, and realistic fiction.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can identify which genre a story belongs to by looking at its features, such as the type of characters, the setting, and the plot.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain what a moral is and give an example from a fable.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can explain the difference between a legend and a fable.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use coordinating conjunctions (FANBOYS) correctly to join two ideas of equal importance.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can use subordinating conjunctions such as because, although, when, if, unless, while, until, after, before, and since to link a main clause with a subordinate clause.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
